content:
Ryan, here’s what I’d do.

- In the service, when Psalm 19 is read, I’d quietly tell my 10-year-old: “Listen to how poetry speaks truly with pictures. ‘The heavens declare’—that’s real truth, even though the sky doesn’t have a mouth. Poetry uses imagery to tell us what is.” Then I’d add, “Our job with every passage is to let God speak in the way He chose—sometimes poetry, sometimes history, sometimes letters. We don’t change the genre to make people happier; we receive it as it is.”

- That afternoon, I’d open the Bible with my child and compare Psalm 19 and Genesis 1. I’d show what poetry looks like (parallel lines, rich imagery) and what narrative looks like (Genesis 1’s repeated refrain, the sequence of events, “and God said…and it was so…and there was evening and morning, the nth day”). I’d connect Exodus 20:8–11 and 31:17, where God grounds the Sabbath in His six days of creation, and remind them Jesus and Paul speak of Adam and the beginning as history (Matt 19:4–5; Rom 5; 1 Cor 15). Then I’d explain: “Poetry can be fully true without being literal in every word. But when the Bible presents events as history, we treat them as history. We don’t call history ‘poetry’ just to avoid trouble.”

- With my elder, I’d ask for a gracious conversation. “Are we saying Genesis 1 is poetic in style or that it isn’t historical? What in the text leads us to that, and how does that square with Exodus 20 and Jesus’/Paul’s references to creation and Adam?” I’d affirm my desire for unity, but also that unity flows from truth (Eph 4:3,13). I’d ask that we teach our people—especially our kids—that genre sensitivity is not the same as genre-switching under cultural pressure. We can acknowledge intramural Christian views on the age of the earth while still refusing to flatten Genesis 1 into ‘mere poetry’ for the sake of optics.

- With my child, I’d close by anchoring them in Psalm 19’s two halves: God’s world truly declares His glory, and God’s Word is perfect and trustworthy. We submit to both, and when there’s tension, we keep studying humbly—but we never bend Scripture to fit the moment. “Let’s let God say what He said, the way He said it.”
